The Concept That Finally Made My React Projects Click
When I first started learning React, I thought importing and exporting were just lines of code I had to write to keep the compiler happy. I knew every file seemed to have an import at the top and an export at the bottom, but I didn’t really understand why they were there.
That changed while building my app using the PokéAPI. Somewhere between organizing components, creating helper functions, and refactoring duplicated code, importing and exporting finally started to make sense. Instead of memorizing syntax, I began seeing how these statements connect different pieces of my application together.
Although I’m demonstrating these ideas through a React project, it’s worth mentioning that import and export aren’t actually React features. They’re part of JavaScript’s module system. React projects rely on them heavily, but you’ll encounter the same syntax in many JavaScript projects outside of React as well.
To make this more concrete, let’s imagine we’re building a React application inspired by Pokémon. Our goal isn’t just to catch six random Pokémon — we’re trying to build a team worthy of challenging the Champion. Whether you picture Cynthia, Steven, or Lance, every great team has one thing in common: each Pokémon has a specific role. React applications work much the same way.
Champion Cynthia’s team from Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum (2009).Breaking the App into Components
One of the first things I learned about React is that it encourages developers to build applications out of components. Instead of putting everything into one massive file, we split a project into smaller, reusable pieces that each have a single responsibility.
When I first started, I wondered why React made this feel so important. As my project grew, the answer became obvious. Searching for Pokémon, displaying stats, building a team, and navigating between pages were all different problems. Keeping them in separate components made the application much easier to understand.
I like to compare it to building a balanced Pokémon team. You wouldn’t expect one Pokémon to handle every opponent or cover every possible matchup. Each member contributes something different, and the same is true for components in an application.
App.js: The Hub of the Application
At the center of my project is App.js. It doesn’t search for Pokémon, display movesets, or manage my saved team by itself. Instead, its job is to organize the application by bringing together the pages responsible for those tasks.
The first thing App.js does is import those components.
import NavBar from "./components/Navbar";
import HomePage from "./pages/HomePage";
import ExplorePage from "./pages/ExplorePage";
import TeamPage from "./pages/TeamPage";These import statements tell JavaScript’s module system — and, in a typical development setup, a build tool such as Webpack or Vite — which modules this file depends on.
Without those imports, names like NavBar, HomePage, and TeamPage would not be defined inside App.js.
Once the components are imported, they can be rendered inside JSX.
<NavBar /><Routes>
<Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
<Route path="/explore" element={<ExplorePage />} />
<Route path="/team" element={<TeamPage />} />
</Routes>This was probably my biggest lightbulb moment. These are not built-in HTML elements. They are components that I created myself.
Importing makes those component values available inside App.js, and React can then use them to render the application’s interface.
So What Does “Export” Mean?
If importing requests code from another JavaScript module, exporting is how that module makes its code available in the first place.
For example, my Navbar.js file ends like this:
export default NavBar;That line makes the NavBar component available to other modules.
Another file can then import it:
import NavBar from "./components/Navbar";The module system and build tool resolve that file path and connect the imported value to the exported component.
Once I understood that relationship, importing and exporting stopped feeling like two unrelated ideas. They are two halves of the same process: one module makes something available, and another module chooses to use it.
Default Exports
Most of the components in my project use default exports because each file is centered around one main component.
For example:
export default HomePage;Another component might end with:
export default PokemonDetails;A module can only have one default export. That makes default exports a natural choice when a file has one primary responsibility.
The exported component can then be imported like this:
import PokemonDetails from "./PokemonDetails";One detail about default imports is that the importing file can technically choose a different local name.
import Details from "./PokemonDetails";That works because the module has one default export. Still, keeping the imported name consistent with the original component name usually makes the project easier to read.
I like to think of the default export as the main role of that file. Everything else inside the module may support it, but that is the primary value the file shares with the rest of the application.
Named Exports
While building my Pokémon-inspired app, I ran into a problem that many developers eventually face: duplicated code.
I had copied the same helper function into multiple components.
function capitalize(word) {
return word.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + word.slice(1);
}The function worked, but keeping multiple copies was not ideal. If I ever changed its behavior, I would have to remember to update every version.
That breaks the DRY principle: “Don’t Repeat Yourself.”
The solution was to move the function into a shared helper file and export it by name.
export function capitalize(word) {
return word.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + word.slice(1);
}Any module that needs the function can import it using curly braces.
import { capitalize } from "../helpers";This small refactor made the project cleaner and easier to maintain. More importantly, it helped me understand why named exports exist. They are useful when a module contains reusable functions, values, or several related pieces of logic.
Exporting Multiple Values from One File
A module can contain more than one named export.
For example:
export function capitalize(word) {
return word.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + word.slice(1);
}export function formatPokemonName(name) {
return name.replace("-", " ");
}Another file can then import both functions.
import {
capitalize,
formatPokemonName,
} from "../helpers";It can also import only one of them.
import { capitalize } from "../helpers";This allows each module to import only the pieces it actually needs.
A single file can also combine a default export with named exports, although I kept my project simpler by mainly using default exports for components and named exports for helper functions.
Default vs. Named Exports
One trick that helped me remember the difference was paying attention to the import statement.
Default imports do not use curly braces.
import PokemonDetails from "./PokemonDetails";Named imports do use curly braces.
import { capitalize } from "../helpers";Eventually I stopped thinking of this as syntax I needed to memorize and started thinking about what the module was exporting.
No braces means:
Give me the module’s default export.
Curly braces mean:
Give me the specific named export with this exact name.
Named imports must match the exported name unless they are explicitly renamed with an alias.
import { capitalize as capitalizeWord } from "../helpers";That imports the exported capitalize function but gives it the local name capitalizeWord.
Seeing the Component Hierarchy
As my project grew, I started seeing the application as a hierarchy of components rather than a collection of unrelated files.
App
├── NavBar
├── HomePage
│ ├── RandomPokemonCard
│ └── RandomPokemonButton
├── ExplorePage
│ ├── SearchBar
│ └── PokemonDetails
│ ├── MoveList
│ ├── EvolutionLine
│ └── SavePokemonForm
└── TeamPageEvery child component must be imported into the module where its parent renders it.
For example, ExplorePage.js imports the components it needs.
import SearchBar from "../components/SearchBar";
import PokemonDetails from "../components/PokemonDetails";It can then render those components and pass data into them through props.
<SearchBar onSearch={searchPokemon} /><PokemonDetails
pokemon={pokemon}
pokedexEntry={pokedexEntry}
evolutionLine={evolutionLine}
/>This separation of responsibilities makes the application easier to understand and maintain. ExplorePage handles searching and fetching data, while PokemonDetails focuses on presenting that information.
The imports and exports make this structure possible, while React handles rendering the components and passing data through props.
A Retrospective
Before building this project, importing and exporting felt like syntax I simply had to memorize.
Now I see them as a major part of how modern JavaScript applications stay organized.
Exports make components, functions, and values available to other modules. Imports bring those values into the files where they are needed. Build tools such as Webpack and Vite resolve those dependencies and package the application so it can run in the browser.
In my React project, this module structure allowed me to separate pages, reuse helper functions, remove duplicated code, and keep each component focused on one job.
Ironically, I started Professor Juniper’s Lab because I wanted to practice working with the PokéAPI. I finished it with a much deeper appreciation for both React’s component architecture and JavaScript’s module system.
Just like building a strong Pokémon team is not about relying on one overpowered member, building a maintainable application is not about writing one enormous file. It is about creating smaller, focused pieces and connecting them together in a clear, reusable structure.
“Vs. Red (On Mt. Silver)” by Serain on DeviantArt.
